findings.description: moving_average uses an incremental sliding sum (current += values[i] - values[i-window]) which accumulates floating-point rounding error across the whole series, so trailing windows drift from a freshly-summed window for long/large-magnitude inputs, contradicting the docstring's unqualified 'trailing moving average' contract.

The incremental running sum permanently poisons all subsequent windows when the series contains inf or NaN: once a +inf/-inf or NaN enters `current`, subtracting it back out yields NaN forever, so every later window is NaN even after the offending value leaves the window (a per-window recompute would recover).
percentile calls sorted(values) with no NaN guard; a NaN in the data silently breaks sorted()'s ordering invariant and produces a wrong, non-monotonic percentile with no error raised.
StreamingMedian.add uses insort, which silently mis-positions NaN values and corrupts the maintained sort order, so median() can return a wrong value with no error once any NaN is added.

clamp returns NaN unchanged when value is NaN (max(low, min(nan, high)) evaluates to nan), silently passing an out-of-range/undefined value through instead of constraining it to [low, high] as the contract implies.

safe_mean propagates NaN silently (any NaN element yields a NaN mean) and offers no separation between 'empty input' (None) and 'contaminated input' (NaN); arguably correct math but a robustness gap for dashboard inputs.

StreamingMedian relies on insort (O(n) shift per insertion) giving O(n^2) total ingestion despite the 'streaming' name; a two-heap structure would be O(log n) per add — out of logic scope, deferred to critic-performance.
